source: MemRL: Self-Evolving Agents via Runtime Reinforcement Learning on Episodic Memory
summary: Non-parametric framework that enables agents to self-evolve through runtime RL on episodic memory using a two-phase retrieval mechanism (semantic relevance + learned Q-values) without updating model weights. Outperforms baselines on BigCodeBench, HLE, ALFWorld, and Lifelong Agent Bench.
relevanceToAgentForge: High — directly inspired HID-127: AgentForge's DistillationAgent only learns from successful runs; MemRL's insight that failed trajectories are equally valuable led to the failure distillation extension.

source: PASTE: Act While Thinking — Pattern-Aware Speculative Tool Execution
summary: Shanghai Jiao Tong + Microsoft Research paper mining execution trace patterns to speculatively execute tools while the LLM is still processing. Achieves 48.5% reduction in task completion time and 1.8x tool throughput improvement.
relevanceToAgentForge: Medium — PASTE targets high-frequency tool-calling agents; AgentForge's batch-oriented CLI subprocess model (one stage at a time, no concurrent tool calls within a stage) doesn't map cleanly onto speculative tool execution. The latency gains would be minor relative to the integration complexity.

summary: Northwestern/Stanford/Cornell/All Hands AI paper demonstrating that frontier LLM agents waste 28-64% of tokens on doomed trajectories because they cannot predict their own failure. Early stop via budget-awareness saves 28-64% tokens on failed runs.
relevanceToAgentForge: High — directly inspired HID-128: AgentForge has no upfront complexity estimation; overscoped plans pass approval and waste implementation compute. The paper's budget-awareness concept maps to a deterministic plan complexity gate before implementation.

source: EvoSkill: Automated Skill Discovery for Multi-Agent Systems
summary: Sentient Labs + Virginia Tech open-source framework (March 2026) that analyzes agent failures and generates reusable skills from failed trajectories, compatible with Claude Code and Codex CLI. Skills are packaged as reusable folders with instructions and metadata.
relevanceToAgentForge: Medium — EvoSkill's core capability (distilling reusable skills from execution traces) is already substantially implemented in AgentForge via DistillationAgent + AgentSkillRepository. The failure-learning gap it addresses was covered by HID-127 instead.

source: Fault-Tolerant Distributed AI Agent Harness: Graceful Degradation Patterns
summary: Zylos Research 2026 synthesis of production fault-tolerance patterns for AI agent systems including tiered retry, circuit breakers, and graceful degradation. Documents median 1.2s recovery time with three-attempt tiered retry and exponential backoff.
relevanceToAgentForge: High — directly inspired HID-126: AgentForge's ProcessRunner throws AgentTimeoutError immediately on timeout with zero retry logic, causing runs to enter AIBlocked on any transient failure.

source: Darwin Godel Machine: Open-Ended Evolution of Self-Improving Agents
summary: Self-improving system that iteratively modifies its own code and validates changes against coding benchmarks, growing an archive of agents via Darwinian selection. Increases SWE-bench from 20% to 50% through open-ended evolution.
relevanceToAgentForge: Medium — DGM's self-modifying code approach is too architectural for AgentForge's current stage; requires running agents that modify the orchestrator itself, which would need significant safety guardrails and a multi-month integration effort.

source: Karpathy AutoResearch: Autonomous ML Experimentation Agent
summary: Karpathy's March 2026 repo (21k stars) giving an AI agent a small LLM training setup to experiment autonomously overnight using a three-file contract system — modify code, train for 5 minutes, evaluate, keep or discard changes.
relevanceToAgentForge: Low — AutoResearch is designed for autonomous ML training experimentation on single-GPU setups; the architecture is specific to model training loops and doesn't generalize to software engineering orchestration workflows.

summary: ICLR 2025 Workshop paper from University of Bristol where agents edit their own prompts and tools based on execution feedback. Demonstrates scaffold-level self-improvement on SWE-bench, pushing performance to 53% by mid-2025.
relevanceToAgentForge: Medium — SICA's prompt-editing capability is largely covered by AgentForge's existing DistillationAgent which extracts reusable skills from completed runs. The self-modification angle would require agents to modify their own prompt templates, a complex trust boundary to manage.
